(born **Mortimer Leon Marlieb**;[[2]](#cite_note-ANB2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) October 17, 1907 – May 22, 1984) was an American actor and theatre director.[[4]](#cite_note-vn-4) He won the [Volpi Cup for Best Actor](/wiki/Volpi_Cup_for_Best_Actor "Volpi Cup for Best Actor") at the [29th Venice International Film Festival](/wiki/29th_Venice_International_Film_Festival "29th Venice International Film Festival") for his performance in [John Cassavetes](/wiki/John_Cassavetes "John Cassavetes")' *[Faces](/wiki/Faces_(1968_film) "Faces (1968 film)")* (1968), and was nominated for an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") and a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") for his role in *[Love Story](/wiki/Love_Story_(1970_film) "Love Story (1970 film)")* (1970).[[5]](#cite_note-NYT2-5) He was also known to film audiences for his role as Jack Woltz—the defiant film mogul who awakens to find the severed head of his prized thoroughbred horse in his bed—in *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* (1972).[[5]](#cite_note-NYT2-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1907–1984)

This article is about the American actor. For the mining engineer and geologist, see [John Marley (mining engineer)](/wiki/John_Marley_(mining_engineer) "John Marley (mining engineer)").

| John Marley | |
| --- | --- |
| [Black-and-white close-up photo of the face of a smiling man with light hair, thick dark eyebrows, and visible stubble.](/wiki/File:John_Marley_(1968,_Faces).jpg) Marley from a poster promoting *[Faces](/wiki/Faces_(1968_film) "Faces (1968 film)")* (1968) | |
| Born | Mortimer Leon Marlieb  (1907-10-17)October 17, 1907  New York City, U.S. |
| Died | May 22, 1984(1984-05-22) (aged 76)  [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, U.S. |
| Resting place | [Cedar Park Cemetery](/wiki/Cedar_Park_Cemetery,_Emerson "Cedar Park Cemetery, Emerson") |
| Alma mater | [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York") |
| Occupations | * Actor * director |
| Years active | 1947–1984 |
| Spouses | * Stanja Lowe  ​  ​ (m. 1951; div. 1971)​ * Sandra Marley  ​ (m. 1975)​ |
| Children | 4[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) |

**John Marley** (born **Mortimer Leon Marlieb**;[[2]](#cite_note-ANB2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) October 17, 1907 – May 22, 1984) was an American actor and theatre director.[[4]](#cite_note-vn-4) He won the [Volpi Cup for Best Actor](/wiki/Volpi_Cup_for_Best_Actor "Volpi Cup for Best Actor") at the [29th Venice International Film Festival](/wiki/29th_Venice_International_Film_Festival "29th Venice International Film Festival") for his performance in [John Cassavetes](/wiki/John_Cassavetes "John Cassavetes")' *[Faces](/wiki/Faces_(1968_film) "Faces (1968 film)")* (1968), and was nominated for an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") and a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") for his role in *[Love Story](/wiki/Love_Story_(1970_film) "Love Story (1970 film)")* (1970).[[5]](#cite_note-NYT2-5) He was also known to film audiences for his role as Jack Woltz—the defiant film mogul who awakens to find the severed head of his prized thoroughbred horse in his bed—in *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* (1972).[[5]](#cite_note-NYT2-5)

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Marley&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

Marley was born in Harlem in New York City to [Russian-Jewish](/wiki/Russian-Jewish "Russian-Jewish") immigrant parents.[[6]](#cite_note-ANB-6) He dropped out of the [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York"), turning to a career in acting.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) He served in the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [Signal Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Signal_Corps "United States Army Signal Corps") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Marley&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Marley&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Film and television")]

Marley was a prolific actor, appearing in nearly 250 films and television series during a career spanning over 45 years. He had roles in TV series that included *[The Web](/wiki/The_Web_(1950_TV_series) "The Web (1950 TV series)")*, *[Peter Gunn](/wiki/Peter_Gunn "Peter Gunn")*, *[Johnny Staccato](/wiki/Johnny_Staccato "Johnny Staccato")*, *[Bourbon Street Beat](/wiki/Bourbon_Street_Beat "Bourbon Street Beat")*, *[Perry Mason](/wiki/Perry_Mason_(1957_TV_series) "Perry Mason (1957 TV series)")*, *[Rawhide](/wiki/Rawhide_(TV_series) "Rawhide (TV series)")*, *[The Untouchables](/wiki/The_Untouchables_(1959_TV_series) "The Untouchables (1959 TV series)")*, *[Sea Hunt](/wiki/Sea_Hunt "Sea Hunt")*, *[77 Sunset Strip](/wiki/77_Sunset_Strip "77 Sunset Strip")*, *[The Lloyd Bridges Show](/wiki/The_Lloyd_Bridges_Show "The Lloyd Bridges Show")*, *[Dr. Kildare](/wiki/Dr._Kildare_(TV_series) "Dr. Kildare (TV series)")*, *[The Outer Limits](/wiki/The_Outer_Limits_(1963_TV_series) "The Outer Limits (1963 TV series)")*, *[The Alfred Hitchcock Hour](/wiki/The_Alfred_Hitchcock_Hour "The Alfred Hitchcock Hour")*, *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1959_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1959 TV series)")*, *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")*, *[The Wild Wild West](/wiki/The_Wild_Wild_West "The Wild Wild West")*, *[Mannix](/wiki/Mannix "Mannix")*, *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")*, *[Ironside](/wiki/Ironside_(1967_TV_series) "Ironside (1967 TV series)")*, *[The Name of the Game](/wiki/The_Name_of_the_Game_(TV_series) "The Name of the Game (TV series)")*, *[The F.B.I.](/wiki/The_F.B.I._(TV_series) "The F.B.I. (TV series)")*, *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")*, *[McCloud](/wiki/McCloud_(TV_series) "McCloud (TV series)")*, *[Kolchak: The Night Stalker](/wiki/Kolchak:_The_Night_Stalker "Kolchak: The Night Stalker")*, *[Baretta](/wiki/Baretta "Baretta")*, *[Barnaby Jones](/wiki/Barnaby_Jones "Barnaby Jones")*, and *[Hawaii Five-0](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*.

He was cast as George Campbell in the 1961 episode "Jerkwater" of the series *[The Rebel](/wiki/The_Rebel_(American_TV_series) "The Rebel (American TV series)")*, starring [Nick Adams](/wiki/Nick_Adams_(actor,_born_1931) "Nick Adams (actor, born 1931)").

In 1962 he played the role of murderer Matthew Owen in the *[Perry Mason](/wiki/Perry_Mason_(1957_TV_series) "Perry Mason (1957 TV series)")* episode "The Case of the Angry Astronaut." He also showed his acting prowess in a supporting but important role of Charlie Rondell in an episode of *[The Virginian](/wiki/The_Virginian_(TV_series) "The Virginian (TV series)")* (1968), titled "The Crooked Path."

Marley also played in two other movies where he attained notoriety. One was the cult movie *The Car*, where he played a sheriff of a small town that was victimized by a mysterious, black automobile.

The second movie was titled *Framed*, where he played mob boss Sal Viccarone, who befriended a gambler while they were both in prison.

One of Marley's more notable roles, albeit short, was that of film producer Jack Woltz in *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")*. Marley later spoofed that role in an episode of *[SCTV Network](/wiki/SCTV_Network "SCTV Network")*. He played Max Berns, a film producer who was a caring father figure to Burt Reynolds in the stuntman tribute *[Hooper](/wiki/Hooper_(film) "Hooper (film)")*.[[7]](#cite_note-NYT-7)

In the late 1970s, he appeared in the third season of the popular television series *[The Incredible Hulk](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(1978_TV_series) "The Incredible Hulk (1978 TV series)")* as D.W. Banner, the father of David Banner in the episode titled "Homecoming".

### Stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Marley&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stage")]

Marley's Broadway credits include *The Investigation* (1966), *Sing Till Tomorrow* (1953), *The Strong Are Lonely* (1953), *Skipper Next to God* (1947),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and *Johnny Doodle* (1942).[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) Elsewhere on stage, Marley appeared in the world premiere production of [Edna St. Vincent Millay](/wiki/Edna_St._Vincent_Millay "Edna St. Vincent Millay")'s poetry drama, *Conversation At Midnight* in 1961 in Los Angeles, in an ensemble cast which included [James Coburn](/wiki/James_Coburn "James Coburn"), [Jack Albertson](/wiki/Jack_Albertson "Jack Albertson") and [Eduard Franz](/wiki/Eduard_Franz "Eduard Franz"). The production was directed by [Robert Gist](/wiki/Robert_Gist "Robert Gist") and produced by [Worley Thorne](/wiki/Worley_Thorne "Worley Thorne") and Susan Davis.

He also directed [Little Theater productions](/wiki/Little_Theatre_Movement "Little Theatre Movement") in several cities.[[4]](#cite_note-vn-4)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Marley&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Marley was twice married. He and his first wife, the actress Stanja Lowe, had three children.[[4]](#cite_note-vn-4)

In 1984, Marley died at age 76 following open-heart surgery.[[9]](#cite_note-WVobit-9) He is interred at [Cedar Park Cemetery](/wiki/Cedar_Park_Cemetery,_Emerson,_New_Jersey "Cedar Park Cemetery, Emerson, New Jersey") in Emerson, New Jersey.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
